This is a fifty-six year old male with a PSA of five and a visual retinal exam showing a small bulge. He is in good health with no prior family history. He has prior surgery with laparoscopy. The robotic surgical system is utilized with three arms and a camera. The surgeon operates the monopolar scissors with the right hand and the bipolar device with the left hand, while the ProGrasp instrument in the third arm provides manipulation and counter-traction of tissues.

The surgeon begins the posterior dissection using a transperitoneal technique, focusing on the precise dissection of the seminal vesicles and vasa. The seminal vesicles are visible with arteries that are clipped to minimize cautery impact on surrounding tissues, particularly where nerves related to sexual function are located.

The patient's sexual function was minimal, but he desired nerve sparing to try to preserve his remaining function. We also used a nerve monitor manufactured by Propep Surgical, which is used during the case in a fashion where we can measure signals from the patient's neurovascular bundles to assess location as well as preservation at the end of the case. Removal at the seminal vesicles does not mean that the patient will not have a lack of erection. By using curable dissection lateral to the seminal vesicles, we are trying to spare as much of the neurovascular tissue as possible in order to try to maximize the patient's chance of recovery afterwards.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles and proceeds to dissect Denonvilliers' fascia, a membranous partition separating the prostate and urinary bladder from the rectum. Cold dissection with scissors is used to carefully separate this fascia, minimizing the risk of rectal injury.

The surgeon uses the left bipolar device to pull on the medial umbilical ligament while dissecting laterally with hot scissors. The mesh from a previous surgery is visible and close to the dissection area, requiring careful navigation through areolar tissue to locate the pubic symphysis and avoid damage to surrounding vasculature.
Thank you. Thank you.

The surgeon mobilizes the anterior bladder wall and proceeds to identify the endophilic fascia. The periprostatic fat surrounding the prostate is carefully removed, facilitating better visualization of the tissues for subsequent dissection. This step is performed smoothly with minimal blood loss.

The robotic-assisted dissection is performed along the levator muscles near the dorsal vein, with careful sparing of individual muscle fibers. Cold dissection is used to minimize damage to muscles and nerve fibers. The surgeon is on the left side of the prostate, cutting across the puboprostatic ligaments to expose the dorsal vein.
The surgeon uses robotic needle drivers to suture the dorsal vein with a zero PDS suture, demonstrating the application of a slip knot to ensure a tight seal and minimize bleeding.

The surgeon performs an anterior urethra pexy as part of the reconstruction after prostate removal, repositioning the urethra to an anterior position to restore its angle and aid in continence recovery.

The surgeon prepares to start the bladder neck dissection, introducing Propep electrodes for nerve monitoring. The electrodes are integrated into the surgical robot and will be used to stimulate nerves around the prostate, providing real-time feedback on nerve proximity and function.

The surgeon performs dissection of the bladder neck, using a technique that involves keeping the Foley balloon inflated and moving it back and forth to help delineate the interface between the prostate and the bladder.

The surgeon carefully opens the bladder neck, minimizing traction to prevent enlargement of the bladder. The dissection proceeds in an areolar tissue plane, indicating the upcoming identification of the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The risk of injury to the posterior bladder neck is reduced.

The surgeon exposes the shiny white surface of the seminal vesicles and vasa, indicating completion of the posterior bladder neck dissection. Attention shifts to the prostate pedicles, where nerve-sparing techniques are initiated using anti-grade and retrograde release of the neurovascular bundles to minimize tension and prevent neuropraxia.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the neurovascular bundles using a combination of sharp, blunt, and pinpoint cautery techniques, staying high above the nerve bundles to ensure a good release of nerve tissues. Nerve monitoring is utilized to confirm the tissue planes during the procedure.

The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, where the site of the prior dorsal vein stitch is visible. Cold irrigation is applied to minimize heat generation from pinpoint cautery, potentially reducing tissue damage during dissection.

The surgeon completes the release of the neurovascular bundles and performs posterior dissection in the area of the previously opened non-VA's fascia. Using sweeping motions, the peri-rectal tissue and neurovascular bundles are moved away from the prostate.

The surgeon applies a hemolog clip to the vascular pedicle after safely removing the neurovascular bundles. Cautery or an additional clip is used to secure the proximal part of the vascular pedicle, minimizing back bleeding and maintaining a clear surgical field.

The surgeon clips the pedicle and continues nerve dissection using pinpoint cautery and small clips to seal veins and small arteries. Copious irrigation is applied to minimize heat transfer during the procedure.

The surgeon performs dissection on the right side of the prostate, releasing the nerve bundles using both antegrade and retrograde techniques. The dissection is carried out with precision to ensure the nerve bundles are freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the right posterior pedicle, adhering to the established principles of careful tissue separation and preservation of surrounding structures.

The surgeon completes the apical dissection using cautery and sharp dissection near the region where the dorsal vein suture was initially applied. Copious irrigation is used to maintain a clear surgical field, ensuring the vascular bundles remain distant from the dissection area. The surgeon pinches the tissue to differentiate it from the prostate.

The surgeon enters the space where the urethra lies, revealing areolar tissue. Traction is applied to the prostate to enhance visualization of the urethra, while nerve bundles are visible running below the bipolar instrument.

The surgeon applies PDS sutures to the dorsal vein to minimize bleeding, creating a bloodless field that enhances the visibility of urethral fibers and helps avoid positive margins during the prostatectomy.
The surgical field is well-visualized with minimal bleeding. The assistant irrigates the area to maintain clarity and prevent mistaking prostate tissue for the urethra.

The surgeon uses cautery to continue dissection, with neurovascular bundles safely out of the operative field. The bloodless field reveals no prosthetic tissue extending underneath the urethra, which is notable in cases with larger prostates.

Following the removal of the prostate, the surgeon observes the vascular bundles on either side of the former prostate location. The urethra is visible with neurovascular bundles merging beneath it. Muscle contractions are seen, and nerve monitoring displays action potentials on a separate screen as the nerve bundles are stimulated.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis using the Van Velthoven technique with two differently colored monocryl sutures to distinguish the left and right sides. Careful coordination with the assistant is visible to avoid damage to the urethra and surrounding nerves.

The surgeon observes the neurovascular bundles running and joining in the midline underneath the urethra, highlighting the area where a posterior reconstruction would typically be performed. The bundles are visible due to effective nerve sparing techniques.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a running suture technique, minimizing tissue handling and reducing the risk of postoperative leakage.

The surgeon completes the final portion of the anastomosis and tests the Foley catheter by injecting 120 cc of sterile saline to check for leaks. The absence of leakage is visually confirmed, reducing the risk of urethral strictures and aiding in the healing of the neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon completes the procedure by dropping the pneumoperitoneum to check for any bleeding. The pouch of Douglas is inspected for bowel injuries and fluid collections, ensuring no unnoticed complications during the robotic radical prostatectomy.
